teaching.notes:
Still life displays:
  Left side — [Left and Right] lighting
  Right side — [Front, Middle, and Back] lighting

--- GRADING SCALE ---
0-4 Scale: 0=F(0%) / 1=D(33%) / 1.5=D+(37.5%) / 2=C(50%) / 2.5=B(62.5%) / 2.8=B+(70%) / 3=A-(75%) / 3.5=A(87.5%) / 4=A+(100%)
Grade %: A=75-100 / B=62-74.9 / C=50-61.9 / D=33-49.9 / F=0-32.9

--- ART RUBRIC ---
Creativity: 4=Super unique / 3=Original ideas / 2=Borrowed ideas / 1=Copied everything
Required Content: 4=All that and some / 3=All of them / 2=Most of them / 1=Nope
Neatness: 4=Perfect / 3=Mostly neat / 2=Almost neat / 1=Messy
Effort: 4=Above and beyond / 3=Good effort / 2=More effort needed / 1=Minimal effort

Teaching Framework: INSPIRE -> DESIGN -> CREATE -> PRESENT & REFLECT

--- INTERVIEW TIPS ---
• Use team talk (we not I)
• Always tie it back to TEAM
• Use real life examples
• Talk about feedback from year one and how I will implement it
• Summarize what was said in the interview
• Say something unique about me

--- LESSON ORGANIZATION TEMPLATE ---
[UNIT] (Element or Principle)
-> [LESSON] (bonus if ties into art style)
-> [SKILL] (worksheet)
-> [PROJECT] (application)
FILE includes: Worksheets/packets / Examples / Lecture notes / Displays / Simplified version* / Master/teacher copies (primes)

Recruiting pitch for next year — document what worked this season.

--- HEAD COACH RESPONSIBILITIES ---
• Attendance / Lettering points / Parent-coach meetings / Timers / Buses / Rosters / Fundraisers / Track funds / Coaching office / Meet workers

--- H-CREW SYSTEM ---
• Need a 10-man crew — one at each hurdle stack
• Preassign athletes — whoever isn't hurdling
• Each hurdle race is right after a distance event so no sprinter has an excuse

--- THE HURDLE COMMANDMENTS ---
• Thou shall not go over a backwards hurdle
• Thou shall not stutter step
• Thou shall clap if a teammate falls
• Thou shall not coward away from a hurdle
• Thou shall keep arms and legs inside the ride at all times

--- STATIC STRETCHES ---
Standing: Legs apart down the middle / Legs apart side to side / Right over left / Left over right / Flamingos
Sitting: Butterfly's / Right leg out left in / Left leg out right in / Pretzels / Figure 4's / Calves (single)

--- SEASON STRUCTURE ---
Pre Season: Conditioning / Circuits / Lifting
Season: Strength work / Speed work / Recovery
Post Season: Specialized workouts / Speed work

--- WEIGHT TRAINING ---
Power/speed based / Olympic lifts / Switch to front squats / Plyos / Medicine ball workouts / Rubber bands

--- COACHING CARD IDEAS ---
Multiple cards: Race plan / Hurdle spaces / Wicket drill / Practice

--- LAND VS SEA EVENTS ---
100 / 200 / 4x50 / 4x100

units.title: Value + Color
units.overview: Value and color control mood, depth, and focus. Learn how light works first, then how color behaves.

projects.name: Pen and Shade Practice
projects.desc: Explore 6 value techniques — hatching, crosshatching, scrumble, stipple, tonal, and smudge.
steps: Grab both Pen & Shade practice worksheets.
In each row, study the example technique shown.
Complete the technique in the practice box.
Apply that technique to the object beside it.
Assume light source is upper left for each object.
Fill in all 6 techniques for full credit.
projects.tip: Techniques: Hatching · Cross-Hatching · Scrumble · Stipple · Tonal · Smudge

projects.name: Depth Illusion Boxes
projects.desc: Create the illusion of depth by applying value to simple box forms.
steps: Draw or use the provided box forms.
Choose a consistent light source (upper left).
Identify light face, mid face, and shadow face.
Shade each face a different value: light, mid, dark.
Add a cast shadow beside each box.
Use at least 3 distinct value steps.
projects.tip: Sharper value contrast = more convincing depth.

projects.name: Color Wheel Studies
projects.desc: Build a complete color wheel demonstrating primary, secondary, and intermediate colors.
steps: Place 3 primaries evenly: Red, Yellow, Blue.
Mix and paint 3 secondaries: Orange, Green, Violet.
Mix and paint 6 intermediate (tertiary) colors.
Keep each section clean and fully filled.
Label primary, secondary, and tertiary in your key.
Show complementary color pairs in a second section.
projects.tip: Mix from lightest to darkest — don't add dark paint to a light puddle.

projects.name: Value + Color Quiz
projects.desc: Demonstrate knowledge of value, shading, and color theory.
steps: Complete all questions on the quiz handout.
Show your work on visual questions.
Turn in when finished.
projects.tip: Review your Color Wheel and Pen & Shade Practice first.

projects.name: Geo Forms Shading Exercise
projects.desc: Shade basic geometric forms showing accurate light source, value transitions, and 3D form.
steps: Use provided sphere, cube, cone, and cylinder.
Choose a consistent light source (upper left).
Apply all 5 zones: highlight, midtone, core shadow, reflected light, cast shadow.
Blend transitions smoothly — avoid harsh lines.
Each form should clearly read as 3-dimensional.
projects.tip: Reflected light = small lighter area on the darkest edge. Light bouncing off the surface below.

projects.name: Tree Shading
projects.desc: Apply value techniques to an organic subject — texture, form, and depth.
steps: Use or observe a tree reference.
Sketch basic structure — trunk, branches, foliage clusters.
Block in darkest values first.
Add midtones, then lighten highlight areas.
Suggest texture without drawing every leaf.
Add a ground shadow beneath the tree.
projects.tip: Think of the tree crown as overlapping spheres. Shade each cluster like a 3D form.

rubric.name: Value Range
rubric.desc: Light to dark range is clear

projects.name: Vektor Painting Lesson
projects.desc: Simplify a subject into bold, flat color areas inspired by digital illustration.
steps: Choose a simple subject: face, animal, or object.
Lightly sketch the main shapes.
Identify 3–4 flat value zones.
Mix a paint color for each zone — do not blend between zones.
Paint each zone as a clean flat shape with hard edges.
Step back: does it read clearly from a distance?
projects.tip: No gradients, no blending — just clean, bold shapes. Commit to the flat shapes!

rubric.name: Readability
rubric.desc: Image reads clearly from a distance

projects.name: Half Face Celebrity Shading
projects.desc: Shade half a portrait using realistic value while maintaining accurate facial proportions.
steps: Choose and print a clear celebrity portrait (front-facing).
Grid both your reference and drawing paper.
Transfer basic proportions using the grid method.
Leave one half of the face blank.
Build value from dark to light using layered pencil strokes.
Blend smoothly around nose, eye, and cheek.
Add subtle background contrast.
projects.tip: Eyes sit at the MIDPOINT of the head — not near the top. Most common mistake!

projects.name: Koonz Dog
projects.desc: Study reflective surfaces by shading simplified balloon-like forms inspired by Jeff Koons.
steps: Research Jeff Koons and his Balloon Dog sculpture.
Sketch the basic balloon tube shapes.
Identify where light hits the shiny surface — sharp highlights.
Apply full value range within each tube form.
Add a sharp bright highlight using eraser or white pencil.
Remember: reflection wraps around each balloon.
projects.tip: Shiny = sharpest highlights AND darkest darks right next to each other.

projects.name: Master Study — Henry Matisse
projects.desc: Analyze and recreate Matisse's use of color, shape, and composition.
steps: Research Henry Matisse — bold color, flat forms, pattern.
Choose one Matisse work to study.
Lightly sketch main compositional shapes.
Identify the bold color palette.
Paint flat color areas — do NOT shade realistically.
Focus on relationships between colors.
Add pattern or decorative elements where appropriate.
projects.tip: "Color does not add a pleasant quality to design — it reinforces it." — Matisse

units.title: Space + Texture
units.overview: Flat paper has no depth — artists create that illusion through deliberate choices. This unit builds those skills through perspective drawing, texture techniques, and collage.

projects.name: Illusion of Space Worksheet
projects.desc: Practice the 6 techniques artists use to create the illusion of depth on a flat surface.
steps: Receive the Illusion of Space worksheet.
Read each technique: overlap, size, placement, detail, value, and color.
Complete each example box using the technique shown.
Label each technique clearly.
Add color or value where it helps show depth.
projects.tip: Overlap is the fastest way to show depth — when one object covers another, it instantly reads as closer.

rubric.name: Technique Accuracy
rubric.desc: Each box demonstrates the correct technique

projects.name: 1 Point Perspective Blocks
projects.desc: Use one-point perspective to draw a set of 3D blocks receding to a single vanishing point.
steps: Draw a horizon line across your paper.
Mark one vanishing point (VP) on the horizon line.
Draw the front face of a box — a simple square or rectangle.
Draw lines from each corner of the front face to the VP.
Close off the back edges to complete the box.
Draw at least 3 boxes at different positions: left, right, and centered.
Erase construction lines and add light shading to show form.
projects.tip: Every receding line must go to the SAME vanishing point — even tiny angles will break the illusion.

projects.name: Space + Texture Quiz
projects.desc: Assess understanding of the illusion of space, perspective, and texture vocabulary.
steps: Complete all questions on the quiz handout.
Answer visual identification questions by labeling diagrams.
Turn in when finished.
projects.tip: Review your Illusion of Space worksheet and Textures Practice before taking this.

projects.name: 1 Point Perspective Name
projects.desc: Draw your name in 1-point perspective block letters receding to a vanishing point.
steps: Lightly draw a horizon line and mark one vanishing point.
Write your name in large block letters across the page.
Draw lines from each corner of every letter to the vanishing point.
Close off the depth edges to complete each 3D letter.
Erase construction lines.
Outline letters in pen or marker.
Add color or shading — lighter on the face, darker on the sides.
projects.tip: Keep your block letters simple and wide — overly thin letters are hard to put in perspective.

projects.name: 2 Point Perspective House
projects.desc: Design and draw a house or building using two-point perspective with accurate construction and detail.
materials: Pen or fine liner for outlines
steps: Draw a horizon line and place two vanishing points near the edges of your paper.
Draw the front corner vertical line — make it tall for a convincing building.
Construct the basic box form of the house using both VPs.
Add a roof: draw a center vertical above the box, connect it to the top corners.
Add windows and a door — all receding lines must go to the VPs.
Add details: trim, shutters, chimney, path, landscaping.
Erase all construction lines.
Add color or shading — 3 values for the 3 visible faces.
projects.tip: Doors and windows are just smaller boxes — use the same VP lines. Everything in 2PP obeys the same two points.

rubric.name: Perspective Accuracy
rubric.desc: All lines converge to correct VPs

rubric.name: Construction
rubric.desc: House is properly built from box form

rubric.name: Detail
rubric.desc: Windows, door, and at least 2 other details

rubric.name: Craftsmanship
rubric.desc: Clean final lines, no construction marks

projects.name: Master Study — Banksy
projects.desc: Recreate or reinterpret a work by Banksy, focusing on how he uses space, silhouette, and implied texture to communicate a message.
steps: Research Banksy — look at 10+ works. Note his use of stencil, negative space, and humor.
Choose one Banksy piece to study (or create your own in his style).
Sketch the composition — Banksy works are often simple: one or two figures plus a background.
Create a stencil effect: either cut an actual stencil or simulate spray paint edges.
Focus on SILHOUETTE — Banksy's figures are often pure black shapes.
Add context: a background, wall texture, or environmental element.
Your piece must communicate a clear idea or message.
projects.tip: Banksy's power is in simplicity. One strong silhouette + one unexpected detail = the whole statement.

rubric.name: Stencil / Silhouette Quality
rubric.desc: Banksy-style silhouette is clear and clean

units.title: Balance
units.overview: Balance is one of the foundational principles of design — the visual weight of a composition and how it makes a viewer feel stable, tense, or dynamic. This unit explores symmetrical, asymmetrical, and radial balance through hands-on projects ranging from design exercises to expressive fine art.

projects.name: Composition Practice
projects.desc: Arrange simple shapes on a page to explore how size, placement, and weight affect visual balance. Try symmetrical, asymmetrical, and radial arrangements.
steps: Arrange simple shapes on a page.
Try a symmetrical arrangement — mirror one side.
Try an asymmetrical arrangement — balance with visual weight, not matching.
Try a radial arrangement — elements radiating from a center point.
Label each composition with the type of balance it demonstrates.
projects.tip: Visual weight isn't just about size — a small dark shape can balance a large light one.

d.4: All 3 types clearly shown and labeled

rubric.name: Visual Weight Awareness
rubric.desc: Intentional use of size, placement, or value

d.4: Clear deliberate control of visual weight

projects.name: Bauhaus Balance Composition
projects.desc: Create a geometric composition inspired by Bauhaus design principles — primary colors, basic shapes, and deliberate use of visual weight and balance.
steps: Research Bauhaus design — look at works by Moholy-Nagy, Albers, Klee.
Limit yourself to circles, squares, triangles, and rectangles only.
Limit your palette to primary colors (red, yellow, blue) plus black and white.
Arrange shapes to demonstrate a clear principle of balance.
Fill the composition edge to edge — no blank center or corner drift.
Title your piece with the type of balance it uses.
projects.tip: Bauhaus = function meets beauty. Every shape should earn its place.

projects.name: Balance Quiz
projects.desc: Assess understanding of symmetrical, asymmetrical, radial, and crystallographic balance — vocabulary, identification, and application.
steps: Complete all written questions on the quiz handout.
Identify balance types in provided composition diagrams.
Answer short-response questions about visual weight and design decisions.
projects.tip: Review your Composition Practice and Bauhaus piece before the quiz.

projects.name: Minimalist Album Cover
projects.desc: Design an album cover using only 2–3 colors and minimal shapes. Focus on achieving strong visual balance without relying on detail.
steps: Invent a band name and album title — these are your only text elements.
Choose a single dominant image or shape.
Sketch 3 thumbnail compositions before committing.
Choose a palette of 2–3 colors maximum.
Construct your final design with clean, precise shapes and lettering.
Balance your text and image.
projects.tip: The best minimalist design removes everything that isn't necessary.

d.4: Truly minimal — every element earns its place

projects.name: Radial Name Design
projects.desc: Write your name in decorative lettering inside one triangle of an 8-fold radial grid, then trace it into all eight sections to create a mandala-style radial symmetry composition.
steps: Draw a large circle and divide it into 8 equal wedges (like a pie).
Design your name in decorative block lettering to fit one wedge.
Trace that wedge into all 8 sections using tracing paper or light box.
Each section should be identical — perfect radial symmetry.
Add color using markers or colored pencils.
projects.tip: Radial symmetry reads as unified and powerful — think mandalas, snowflakes, wheels.

projects.name: Abstract Oil Pastel Design
projects.desc: Create an expressive abstract composition using oil pastels that demonstrates a clear principle of balance — let color weight, shape, and placement do the work.
steps: Choose a principle of balance: symmetrical, asymmetrical, or radial.
Block in large color areas first — don't start with detail.
Think about visual weight: dark colors and large shapes carry more weight.
Layer oil pastels — blend where you want transitions, leave hard edges where you want contrast.
Step back and evaluate: does the composition feel resolved?
projects.tip: In abstract work, the viewer can't rely on subject matter — composition and color weight have to carry everything.

d.4: Sophisticated use of color as weight

projects.name: Asymmetrical Balance Collage / Painting
projects.desc: Create a large-format mixed media piece — collage and/or paint — that achieves clear asymmetrical balance. Different visual weights on each side of the composition must feel resolved, not accidental.
materials: Magazines or printed images for collage
steps: Sketch 3 thumbnail compositions exploring asymmetrical balance.
Choose your strongest thumbnail and scale it up.
Block in large background color areas with paint.
Layer collage elements — consider texture, value, and scale as visual weight tools.
Add painted details or marks on top of collage.
Final piece should feel balanced but NOT symmetrical — different on each side, resolved overall.
projects.tip: Asymmetrical balance is the hardest to pull off. The two sides should feel like equal weight even though they look completely different.

rubric.name: Asymmetrical Balance
rubric.desc: Both sides feel equal weight, look different

rubric.name: Visual Weight Control
rubric.desc: Deliberate use of size, color, and texture

projects.name: Master Study — Josef Albers
projects.desc: Study and recreate a work from Albers' Homage to the Square series, then design your own variation. Focus on how color relationships, proportion, and placement create optical balance and visual tension.
steps: Research Josef Albers and his Homage to the Square series.
Choose one painting from the series to recreate exactly.
Use a ruler and tape to mask off the nested square composition precisely.
Mix your colors carefully — match the Albers piece as closely as you can.
Paint each square as a perfectly flat, even area of color.
Remove tape carefully once dry.
Design your own Homage to the Square variation — new color relationships, your choice.
Write 2–3 sentences: how do the colors interact differently in your version?
projects.tip: Albers proved that the same color looks completely different depending on what surrounds it.

d.4: Precise, clean match to Albers original

rubric.name: Color Mixing
rubric.desc: Colors closely match the chosen work

units.title: Heads + Self Portraits
units.overview: The human face is one of the most observed and least accurately drawn subjects in art. This unit replaces assumptions with structure, using the Loomis method to build proportion and feature placement from the ground up.

projects.name: Facial Feature Worksheet
projects.desc: Study and practice drawing individual facial features — eyes, nose, mouth, and ears — in isolation before combining them.
steps: Section 1 — Eyes: Draw a front-view eye, then a 3/4 view eye. Focus on the tear duct, iris shape, and eyelid thickness.
Section 2 — Nose: Draw a front-view nose and a 3/4 view nose. Think of the nose as 3 spheres and a base.
Section 3 — Mouth: Draw lips in rest position, then a slight smile. Focus on the cupid's bow and the shadow under the lower lip.
Section 4 — Ear: Draw a front-view ear. Note the inner ridges — helix, antihelix, and lobe.
Add light shading to each feature to show basic form.
projects.tip: Study features separately before putting them together. Most proportion errors come from guessing instead of observing.

rubric.name: Eye Accuracy
rubric.desc: Front and 3/4 view eyes drawn correctly

rubric.name: Nose Accuracy
rubric.desc: Front and 3/4 view nose drawn correctly

d.4: Accurate with inner ridges and shading

projects.name: Loomis Head Drawing
projects.desc: Use the Loomis method to construct a proportionally accurate head from a sphere, a plane cut, and a centerline.
steps: Draw a circle — this is the cranial mass.
Slice off a flat plane on one side to create the cheekbone/jaw area.
Add a vertical centerline and horizontal brow line to establish the face direction.
Place the brow line, nose line (halfway between brow and chin), and mouth line (1/3 above chin).
Add eyes on the brow line — one eye-width apart.
Add the nose, mouth, and ears using your measured lines.
Draw at least 2 heads: one front view, one 3/4 view.
projects.tip: The eyes are at the MIDPOINT of the head — not near the top. Measure before you place them.

d.4: Both heads show clear Loomis construction

rubric.name: Craftsmanship
rubric.desc: Clean construction lines and finished drawing

projects.name: Loomis + Head Quiz
projects.desc: Assess understanding of the Loomis method, facial proportions, and feature placement.
steps: Complete all written questions on the quiz.
Label the diagram sections with correct facial proportion landmarks.
Draw a small Loomis head from memory in the blank section.
projects.tip: If you can draw a Loomis head from scratch without looking, you'll ace this.

projects.name: The Selfie Project
projects.desc: Create a fully realized self-portrait that communicates something true about your identity — not just what you look like, but who you are.
materials: Selfie reference photo (printed or on device)
Any additional media of your choice
steps: Take or choose a selfie that feels like YOU — consider angle, expression, and setting.
Sketch the portrait using the Loomis method for accurate proportions.
Decide on your medium: pencil, paint, mixed media, collage, etc.
Incorporate at least one personal element beyond the face: background, objects, patterns, text.
Refine your value structure — light source should be consistent.
Final piece should feel personal, not just technical.
projects.tip: The best self-portraits tell you something about the person you couldn't learn from a photo.

rubric.name: Technical Skill
rubric.desc: Medium used with control and intention

projects.name: Master Study — Jean-Michel Basquiat
projects.desc: Study and recreate a work by Basquiat, focusing on his use of raw mark-making, text, symbols, and expressive portraiture.
materials: Markers, oil pastel, or crayon for text and marks
steps: Research Jean-Michel Basquiat — look at 10+ works. Note his use of text, crowns, crossed-out words, and raw line.
Choose one Basquiat portrait work to study.
Sketch the basic composition — don't overthink it. Basquiat worked fast and raw.
Block in the main colors. Basquiat used bold, clashing color.
Add text, symbols, and mark layers on top of the paint.
Cross out words, add arrows, repeat shapes — this is part of his visual language.
Don't over-refine. Rough edges and visible struggle are part of the style.
projects.tip: Basquiat's work is about identity, history, and power. Your study should feel alive, not polished.

rubric.name: Text & Symbol Use
rubric.desc: Words and marks integrated into composition

units.title: Body + Character Design
units.overview: Figure drawing is the foundation of character design. This unit builds proportion and figure skills through the 8-head model, then applies them to original character work, costume design, and full production pieces. You'll start with structure and proportion, then push into invention — designing original characters with consistent anatomy, personality, and style.

projects.name: 8 Head Model Practice
projects.desc: Use the 8-head proportion system to construct a full standing figure. Divide the figure into 8 equal head-height units and place landmarks at their correct positions. Complete at least 3 practice figures with visible guidelines.
steps: Draw a vertical line and divide it into 8 equal units — each unit = one head height.
Mark landmarks: Head (1), Chest (2), Navel (3), Crotch (4), Mid-thigh (5), Knee (6), Mid-shin (7), Feet (8).
Sketch the basic body form over your measurement guide.
Complete at least 3 practice figures — keep the guidelines visible.
Check: arms reach mid-thigh, shoulders are 2 heads wide.
projects.tip: The 8-head model is a guide, not a cage. Real bodies vary — but knowing the standard lets you break it intentionally.

rubric.name: Landmark Placement
rubric.desc: Shoulders, hips, knees, feet at correct positions

projects.name: Body Parts Worksheet
projects.desc: Isolated practice of the parts that trip people up most — hands, feet, and torsos. Draw each section multiple times from reference, focusing on structure and form.
materials: Body Parts worksheet or blank paper
steps: Section 1 — Hands: Draw an open hand front view, then a fist. Focus on the knuckle structure and finger proportions.
Section 2 — Feet: Draw a foot from the side, then from the front. Think of the foot as a wedge shape.
Section 3 — Torso: Draw a front torso and a 3/4 torso. Block in the ribcage and pelvis as separate masses.
Add light shading to each to show form, not just outline.
Complete at least 2 drawings per section.
projects.tip: Think of each body part as a 3D form, not a flat outline. Hands are boxes. Feet are wedges. The torso is two interlocking cylinders.

d.4: Both accurate with wedge form evident
Both show ribcage/pelvis as separate masses

projects.name: Silhouette Lineup
projects.desc: Design 5–6 character silhouettes that are instantly readable and visually distinct from one another using only solid black shapes — no internal lines, no detail.
steps: Design 5–6 character concepts — think about their role, size, and personality.
For each character, fill in a complete solid black silhouette — no outlines, no detail inside.
Each silhouette must read clearly as a unique character at arm's length.
If you can't tell them apart as silhouettes, the design isn't working yet — push it further.
Write the character's role or name beside each silhouette.
projects.tip: Shape language is everything: round = friendly, angular = dangerous, wide = strong, thin = fast. Let the shape do the storytelling.

rubric.name: Readability
rubric.desc: Each silhouette reads clearly as a character

projects.name: Body Comp Quiz
projects.desc: Assess understanding of the 8-head proportion model, figure landmark placement, and character design vocabulary. Includes identification, short answer, and a timed proportion sketch.
steps: Label the 8 landmark positions on a provided figure diagram.
Answer short written questions about proportion and figure structure.
Answer vocabulary identification questions on character design terms.
Draw a quick 8-head proportion guide from memory in the blank section.
projects.tip: If you can place all 8 landmarks without looking and sketch a full figure in under 5 minutes, you'll ace this.

projects.name: Original Character Design
projects.desc: Design an original character from scratch. Start with a silhouette, then develop proportion, costume, and personality details. Your final sheet should include a front-facing figure built on the 8-head model, at least one detailed close-up, and a short written description.
steps: Start with a silhouette — design the shape before the details.
Build the full front-facing figure using the 8-head proportion guide.
Add costume and accessories that tell us who this character is.
Draw at least one detailed close-up: face, hands, or a signature item.
Write 2–3 sentences describing who this character is: name, role, personality.
Ink and color your final character.
projects.tip: The best character designs are readable as a silhouette alone. Design outside-in.

d.4: Name, role, and personality clearly written

projects.name: Character in 4 Styles
projects.desc: Take one character — yours or a provided one — and redraw it in 4 distinct cartoon or comic styles. Each version should feel authentically like that style, not just a slight tweak.
steps: Choose a character to use — your Original Character Design works great here.
Choose 4 distinct styles: e.g. Disney, anime, Looney Tunes, superhero comic, chibi, noir.
Research each style before you draw — look at 5+ examples per style.
Redraw the character in each style. Push the style hard — subtle tweaks don't count.
Label each version with its style name.
projects.tip: Ask yourself what actually DEFINES each style — line weight, head size, eye shape, body proportion. Then exaggerate those things.

rubric.name: Style Authenticity
rubric.desc: Each version reads as that specific style

d.1: Hard to tell it's the same character

projects.name: Costume Design Variants
projects.desc: Take a single character body and design 3 completely different outfits that each tell a different story about who that character is.
steps: Draw the same character body pose 3 times — you can trace or lightly block in the same figure.
Design 3 completely different outfits that each communicate a different personality or context.
Each costume should make the character feel like a completely different person.
Ink and add flat color to each variant.
Label each variant with the personality or role it communicates.
projects.tip: Same body, totally different personality. The costume is doing the characterization work here.

d.4: Dramatically different — each a distinct personality
Each costume immediately communicates a role or personality

projects.name: Comic Strip
projects.desc: Create a multi-panel comic strip (minimum 4 panels) featuring your original character. Figures must demonstrate consistent proportions across panels, dynamic poses, and clear storytelling through composition and expression.
materials: Comic strip paper or heavy paper divided into panels
steps: Plan your story first — write a 1-sentence summary: who, what, where, and why it matters.
Thumbnail all panels in tiny sketches before drawing full size.
Each panel should have a clear purpose — establish, action, reaction, resolution.
Vary your panel sizes — close-ups for emotion, wide shots for action.
Draw figures using the 8-head model.
Add speech bubbles and captions.
Ink all panels. Add color or value to finish.
projects.tip: Comics are about what you leave OUT as much as what you include. Every panel earns its place.

rubric.name: Visual Storytelling
rubric.desc: Story is clear without needing explanation

rubric.name: Panel Composition
rubric.desc: Varied panel sizes and camera angles

rubric.name: Figure Drawing
rubric.desc: Consistent proportions across panels, dynamic poses

rubric.name: Lettering & Dialogue
rubric.desc: Text is legible and placed well

projects.name: Emotional Figure Painting
projects.desc: Pick an emotion, a mood, or a moment — something you've actually felt. Use the body as your subject but let go of accuracy. Exaggerate the pose, push the color, let the paint be messy if it needs to be. The goal isn't a perfect figure — it's a painting that actually feels like something.
steps: Choose your emotion or moment. Be specific — not "sad" but "the feeling after a bad day when you're sitting in your car."
Find or create a reference pose that physically embodies that feeling.
Sketch loosely — don't be precious about accuracy.
Exaggerate: make the pose more extreme, the colors more intense, the marks more expressive.
Let the medium be part of the expression — drips, texture, and mess can be intentional.
Ask yourself: does this actually FEEL like something when you look at it?
projects.tip: This is the only project in this unit where accuracy doesn't matter. Expression does.

d.4: Viewer can feel it without being told what it is

rubric.name: Expressive Use of Medium
rubric.desc: Paint, marks, or color used expressively

d.4: Medium actively contributes to the emotion
Body language is the main storyteller

projects.name: Master Study — Jack Kirby
projects.desc: Study and recreate a panel or figure from Jack Kirby's work, focusing on his dynamic figure construction, exaggerated proportions, and use of geometric form. Follow with your own figure in Kirby's style.
steps: Research Jack Kirby — look at Fantastic Four, New Gods, Captain America. Study 10+ panels.
Choose one full panel or figure to recreate exactly.
Lightly sketch the composition — note the exaggerated foreshortening and massive geometric forms.
Look for the "Kirby Krackle" — the pattern of black dots he uses to show energy and cosmic power.
Ink with bold, confident lines. Vary your line weight heavily.
Add spot black aggressively — Kirby used heavy shadow for drama.
Design your own original character or scene in Kirby's style.
Write 2–3 sentences: what makes Kirby's figures feel so powerful?
projects.tip: Kirby drew like the figures were about to explode off the page. Every muscle is a geometric form. Don't be timid.

d.4: Strong match — energy, proportion, and black placement
All key elements present and confident

rubric.name: Written Reflection
rubric.desc: Analysis of what makes the panel work

Fill [SHELL] placeholders — Art Direction, Asset Library, Production sub-tabs
